coredata.dc:title: Determination of dihydralazine based on chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer of hollow carbon nanodots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Suqin
Fan, Zheyan
Chen, Xiaoxia
Wu, Yunfang
Wang, Jianbo
coredata.dc:description: The famous weak chemiluminescence (CL) system of potassium permanganate and sodium bisulfite (KMnO4-HSO3 −) was enhanced by the hollow fluorescent carbon nanodots (HCNs). The investigation of mechanism revealed that the enhanced CL was induced by the excited-state HCNs (HCNs⁎), which could be produced from the electron-transfer annihilation of positively charged HCNs (HCNs +) and negatively charged HCNs (HCNs −) as well as by CL resonance energy transfer (CRET) from excited SO2 (SO2 ⁎)/1O2 to HCNs. The dihydralazine sulfate (DHZS) had a diminishing effect on the CL of HCNs-KMnO4-HSO3 − system due to the competitive consumption of O2 −. Under the optimal conditions, the reduced CL signal with the concentration of DHZS was linear in the range of 1.0×10−7–7.0×10−5 mol/L with a detection limit of 3.0×10−8 mol/L. The relative standard deviation for seven repeated determination of 5.0×10−6 mol/L DHZS was 2.1%. The established method was applied to the determination of DHZS in pharmaceutical preparations, human urine and plasma samples with good precision and accuracy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer
Dihydralazine sulfate
Potassium permanganate
Sodium bisulfite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Dynamic Profiles of the CL Reaction
Determination of DHZS in Real Samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the flow-injection CL manifold. P: peristaltic pump; PMT: photomultiplier tube; BPCL: ultraweak luminescence analyzer.

The CL dynamic profiles for (a) KMnO4-HCNs, (b) KMnO4-DHZS, (c) KMnO4-HSO3 −-DHZS, (d) KMnO4-HSO3 −, (e) KMnO4-HSO3 −-HCNs-DHZS and (f) KMnO4-HSO3 −-HCNs. Conditions: 6.0×10−5 mol/L KMnO4 in 0.2mol/L H2SO4 solution, 6.0×10−4 mol/L HSO3 −, 0.02mol/L HCNs and 5.0×10−6 mol/L DHZS.

(A) The CL signal-time curves, (B) calibration curve for the DHZS concentration in the range of 1.0×10−7–7.0×10−5 mol/L. Concentrations of DHZS in (A): a: 0, b: 1.0×10−7 mol/L, c: 5.0×10−7 mol/L, d: 1.0×10−6 mol/L, e: 9.0×10−6 mol/L, f: 1.5×10−5 mol/L, g: 3.0×10−5 mol/L and h: 7.0×10−5 mol/L.

CL spectra (A) and PL spectra (B). a: KMnO4-HSO3 –-HCNs and b: KMnO4-HSO3 –-HCNs-DHZS. Conditions: 6.0×10–5 mol/L KMnO4 in 0.2 mol/L H2SO4 solution, 6.0×10–4 mol/L HSO3 −, 0.02 mol/L HCNs and 1.0×10–6 mol/L DHZS.
The comparison of the proposed method with the reported methods for the determination of DHZS.
Results of the determination of DHZS in tablets (n =5).
Determination of DHZS in spiked urine and plasma samples (n =5).

head.ce:title: Determination of dihydralazine based on chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer of hollow carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Magnetic Molecules & Magnetic Information Materials Ministry of Education, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041004, Shanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Magnetic Molecules & Magnetic Information Materials Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041004, Shanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Material Science

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: S. Han, Key Laboratory of Magnetic Molecules & Magnetic Information Materials Ministry of Education, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041004, Shanxi, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Magnetic Molecules & Magnetic Information Materials Ministry of Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The famous weak chemiluminescence (CL) system of potassium permanganate and sodium bisulfite (KMnO4-HSO3 −) was enhanced by the hollow fluorescent carbon nanodots (HCNs). The investigation of mechanism revealed that the enhanced CL was induced by the excited-state HCNs (HCNs⁎), which could be produced from the electron-transfer annihilation of positively charged HCNs (HCNs +) and negatively charged HCNs (HCNs −) as well as by CL resonance energy transfer (CRET) from excited SO2 (SO2 ⁎)/1O2 to HCNs. The dihydralazine sulfate (DHZS) had a diminishing effect on the CL of HCNs-KMnO4-HSO3 − system due to the competitive consumption of O2 −. Under the optimal conditions, the reduced CL signal with the concentration of DHZS was linear in the range of 1.0×10−7–7.0×10−5 mol/L with a detection limit of 3.0×10−8 mol/L. The relative standard deviation for seven repeated determination of 5.0×10−6 mol/L DHZS was 2.1%. The established method was applied to the determination of DHZS in pharmaceutical preparations, human urine and plasma samples with good precision and accuracy.

The CL of KMnO4-HSO3 − was enhanced by HCNs. The enhanced mechanism CL was induced by HCNs⁎, which produced from the electron-transfer annihilation and CRET. The competitive consumption of O2 – by DHZS inhibited CL intensity. The method was applied to the determination of DHZS in pharmaceutical preparations and human fluid samples.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Chemiluminescence (CL) is defined as the emission of light from an electronically excited state species which is produced during the course of a chemical reaction. Because the reaction rate and the intensity of emitted light are function of the chemical concentration, CL techniques are suitable for quantitative analysis. As the technique simultaneously comprises kinetic and luminescence features, it provides highly sensitive and wide dynamic ranges, and if the CL quantum yield is high enough, excellent detection limits can be reached. Compared to photoluminescence (PL) process, no external light source is required, which offers some advantages such as the absence of scattering or background PL signal, the absence of problems related to instability of the external source, reduction of interferences due to a nonselective excitation process, and simple instrumentation. Therefore, CL methods have been extended to be a powerful and important tool in a wide range of fields [1–3].

Nanomaterials with the unique chemical and physical properties and high surface area have the capability to amplified the CL signal, which improved the sensitivity and stability of various CL systems and then extended the field of applications of CL detection [4,5]. Colloid nonmetal nanodots, such as carbon nanodots, nitrogen nanodots, silicon nanodots and so on, could be classified as the new zero-dimensional member in the versatile nanomaterial family and they can be produced readily as water-soluble forms by proper bonding with hydrophilic functional group [6–8]. Owing to their extremely small size, PL and their interesting electro-optical properties, the newly emerged nonmetal nanodots have attracted tremendous consideration and possible applications [9–11].

Carbon nanodots were not of pure carbon composition like carbon nanotubes or carbon nanodiamonds, which were called “carbogenic” [12]. They generally consist of carbon, hydrogen and oxygen with sizes below 10nm, in which the carbon shows the character of crystalline graphite [13]. Compared to fluorescent semiconductor nanocrystals, carbon nanodots are superior in chemical stability, biocompatibility, and low toxicity [14]. Analytical applications of CL detection systems assisted by carbon nanodots have attracted the attention of chemists over the past years [15]. Carbon nanodots can participate in CL reaction as the CL emitting species or catalysts. Especially for weak CL systems, the addition of carbon nanodots overcome the small quantum efficiencies and small amount of light emission questions. Carbon nanodots exhibited the enhancing CL property for H2O2-induced ultraweak CL systems [16–18] and Ce(IV)-thiosulfate system [19], which expanded the application of carbon nanodots and the development of CL techniques.

In 1975, Stauff and co-workers [20] reported that the oxidation of HSO3 − by KMnO4 was accompanied by a weak CL emission, and the weak CL of the KMnO4-HSO3 − system produced from excited sulfur dioxide [21,22]. The energy of excited SO2 molecule (ca. 57kcal/mol<500nm) was easily transferred to a fluorescent molecule intentionally added to the system. So far, the known CL system of KMnO4-HSO3 − had been exploited for a variety of analytical applications after enhancing by the presence of fluorescers and non-fluorescers [23,24], and the resultant emission from the fluorescer (sensitizer) was more intense than in its absence [25,26]. However, the enhancing effect of carbon nanodots on CL reaction involving KMnO4-HSO3 − has seldom been studied.

Dihydralazine sulfate (DHZS) is a potent vasodilator drug, and is used to treated the hypertension and heart failure in clinical. Overuse can cause blood pressure drop, heart rate increase and even shock. The adverse reactions of DHZS are diarrhea, palpitations, tachycardia, headaches, vomiting and nausea. So oral administration of drug dosage is important. Several methods have been reported for the determination of DHZS in pharmaceutical preparations, such as spectrophotometry [27,28], CL method [29,30] and chromatography [31].

In this work, using the KMnO4-HSO3 − CL reaction as a model system, the effect of water-soluble hollow fluorescent carbon nanodots (HCNs) on the CL was explored. It was found that HCNs could enhance the CL of the KMnO4-HSO3 − system. The enhanced mechanism of HCNs on the KMnO4-HSO3 − CL system was investigated based on the CL spectra, fluorescence spectra, UV–vis spectra and the effects of radical scavengers on CL intensity. The results demonstrated that CL resonance energy transfer (CRET) occurred between HCNs (as the energy acceptor) and excited state SO2 (as the energy donor), which gave us new insight into the optical properties of the new emerging carbon nanomaterial. HCNs were potential alternatives to traditional CL emitters in KMnO4-HSO3 − system. Moreover, it was found that DHZS exerted a diminishing effect on the enhancement of CL. In this way, a linear relationship between the logarithm of DHZS concentration and the CL intensity produced from the HCNs-KMnO4-HSO3 − system was found. This CL system had been developed as a sensitive and convenient method for the determination of DHZS in pharmaceutical preparation, human urine and plasma samples.

Glacial acetic acid was obtained from Xi'an Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shannxi, China). Phosphorus pentoxide (P2O5) was purchased from Luoyang Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Henan, China). DHZS was purchased from the National Institute for the Control of Pharmaceutical and Biological Products (Beijing, China). KMnO4 and NaHSO3 was purchased from Tianjin Shentai Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Doubly distilled deionized water was used throughout the experiment.

Stock solutions of DHZS (1.0×10−3 mol/L) were prepared by dissolving appropriate amount DHZS in 25mL water. Stock solution of NaHSO3 (0.1mol/L) was prepared in water. The stock solution of 1.0×10−3 mol/L KMnO4 was prepared by dissolving quantitative KMnO4 with boiled and cooled water.

The CL analysis experiments were carried out with an IFFM-D flow injection chemiluminescence analyzers (Xi'an Remax Analytical Instrument Co. Ltd., China). CL spectra were drawn by a BPCL luminescence analyzer with high-energy cutoff filters. UV–vis absorption spectra were taken on Cary 5000 Spectrophotometer (Varian, USA). Fluorescence spectra were obtained by means of Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was carried out on a Varian 660-IR spectrometer in the range of 500–4000cm−1 (Varian, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was recorded by a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan).

The HCNs were synthesized under self-heating, autocatalysis and no stirring conditions according to the published method [32] with some modifications. 1mL of glacial acetic acid and 80μL of water were quickly injected into the 2.5g of P2O5. The color of the mixture changed to brown within a few seconds, and the volume of the sticky slurry mixture was dramatically expanded by foaming because of the boiling of acetic acid by the spontaneous heat from the reaction of P2O5 with water. The reaction lasted for about 5min until exhaustion of the P2O5, then gradually cooled to room temperature within 10min. Finally, the HCNs in the dark brown mixture were collected by dispersing in water and neutralized to pH≈7 by NaOH for use. The reaction of water with P2O5 is highly exothermic, and the released heat was utilized to promote the carbonization reaction besides vaporizing the acetic acid. The self-heated mixture was greatly foamy because of the boiling acetic acid. Continuous heat was produced, as the water was replenished by the carbonization reaction. Sufficient water brought more heat to produce HCNs. The obtained HCNs solution was stable for at least one month in the refrigerator. The HCNs concentration was 0.35mol/L calculated by C element in glacial acetic acid.

A schematic diagram of the flow injection CL system was shown in Fig. 1 . Water was used as carrier to carry the mixture of HSO3 − solution and HCNs solution via the sample injection valve to mix with DHZS solution and KMnO4 solution successively. The solutions of HSO3 −, HCNs, H2O, DHZS and KMnO4 were pumped into the flow cell by two peristaltic pumps at the rate of 2.0mL/min, respectively. The CL signals were monitored by the photomultiplier tube (PMT) adjacent to the flow CL cell, and imported to the computer for data acquisition. Determination of DHZS was performed based on the net CL intensity of ΔI = I 0 − I s, where I 0 and I s denote the CL intensity in the absence and presence of DHZS, respectively.

Two different compound DHZS tablets (sulfate dihydralazine 10mg, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5mg, reserpine 0.1mg) were purchased from local market. The average tablet weight was calculated from the weight of 10 tablets. They were then finely powdered and homogenized, and a portion of the powder equivalent to the average tablet weight of DHZS was accurately weighed and dissolved with water in 100mL volumetric flask. The resultant solution was filtered, the primary filtrate was discarded, and 25mL continuous filtrate transferred into another 100mL flask and diluted to the mark with water. This solution was further diluted with water appropriately so that the final DHZS concentration was within the calibration curve range, and then analyzed according to the procedure described above.

The urine samples were supplied by two healthy volunteers and plasma samples were obtained from our university hospital. A right amount of DHZS standard solution was added to 2mL of urine or plasma, and 2mL of acetonitrile was added for deproteination, followed by centrifugation at 6000rpm for 10min. The supernatant was transferred into a volumetric flask and removed the acetonitrile under the stream of nitrogen, and then was diluted to 50mL with water. The resulting solutions were subjected to the analytical procedure as described above. The blank samples were treated the drug-free urine or plasma samples in the same way.

The UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of the HCNs were shown in Fig. S1A. The curve a was the absorption spectrum of HCNs and exhibited a broad absorption around 297nm and a sharp absorption at 247nm. The broadened peak at 300nm may be attributed to n–π* transitions of CO [33] while the peak at 247nm is likely originated from the formation of multiple polyaromatic chromophores [34,35]. The curve c was the emission spectrum and the maximum was 500nm at around 400nm excitation. The curve b was the excitation spectrum at 500nm emission. Interestingly, the emission spectrum of the HCNs had no shift as the excitation wavelength varied, which was quite different from many previous reports [16–19]. This phenomenon may be caused by fluorescence resonance energy transfer [32]. In the FT-IR spectrum of the HCNs (Fig. S1B), the absorption peak at about 3449cm−1 was formed by the –OH group while the absorption at 1658cm−1 arised from the CO group conjugated with aromatic carbons, which showed that the HCNs were rich in carboxylic groups and unsaturated carbon bonds formed during the carbonization process. A peak of the conjugated CC stretching vibration at 1542cm−1 further indicated the formation of unsaturated carbon bonds in the carbonization process. Fig. S1C showed the TEM images of the as-prepared cross-linked HCNs at low magnification, and the overall sizes of HCNs were about 100nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic Profiles of the CL Reaction
ce:section.ce:para: The dynamic profiles of KMnO4-HSO3 − with and without HCNs or in the absence and presence of DHZS were studied by batch type CL detection system (Fig. 2 ). Results demonstrated that the CL reactions were very quick. After the CL reaction began, the CL intensity reached a maximum at about 1s for the KMnO4-HSO3 − system (curve e) and the KMnO4-HSO3 −-HCNs system (curve f). Obviously, the CL intensity of the KMnO4-HSO3 −-HCNs system was far stronger than that of the KMnO4-HSO3 − system, which indicated the great sensitization of HCNs for the KMnO4-HSO3 − CL system. A significantly reduced CL signal occurred when a small volume of DHZS solution was added to the KMnO4-HSO3 −-HCNs reaction (curve d). The net CL intensity was evident enough, which could improve the accuracy and sensitivity of analysis. The reaction of KMnO4 with DHZS or HCNs had a weak CL emission (curve a and b). The DHZS had a small diminution for the CL intensity of KMnO4-HSO3 − reaction (curve c and e), which was poor for the determination of DHZS.

The reaction conditions were optimized for the KMnO4-HSO3 −-HCNs CL system as shown in Fig. S2. The effect of the KMnO4 concentration was investigated in the range 1.0×10−5–9.0×10−5 mol/L. The CL intensity increased with KMnO4 concentration in the range of 1.0×10−5–6.0×10−5 mol/L and then decreased above 6.0×10−5 mol/L. Consequently, 6.0×10−5 mol/L KMnO4 was chosen as the optimum for further experiments (Fig. S2A). The effect of the HSO3 − concentration on the CL was examined from 1.0×10−4 to 9.0×10−4 mol/L. The result was shown in Fig. S2B. The maximal signal was obtained at 6.0×10−4 mol/L. A higher concentration of HSO3 − did not produce a higher net CL intensity. Therefore, 6.0×10−4 mol/L HSO3 − was selected for subsequent measurements. The effect of HCNs concentration on the CL was studied in the range from 3.5×10−3 to 3.5×10−2 mol/L. the CL signal increased with the concentration of HCNs from 3.5×10−3 to 2.1×10−2 mol/L. Higher concentration resulted in an obvious decrease in CL response. Hence, 0.02mol/L HCNs was recommended (Fig. S2C). The reaction of KMnO4 with HSO3 − in acidic medium and H2SO4 was used to provide an acidic environment. As shown in Fig. S2D, the CL intensity increased with the H2SO4 concentration and reached maximum CL emission by the H2SO4 concentration up to 0.2mol/L. With higher concentration of H2SO4, the CL intensity decreased.

Considering the CL intensity and the consumption of the reagents, the optimum conditions for the detection of DHZS were as follows: 6.0×10−5 mol/L KMnO4 in 0.2mol/L H2SO4 solution, 6.0×10−4 mol/L HSO3 − and 0.02mol/L HCNs.

Under optimum conditions, a series of solutions containing DHZS were used to construct the calibration curve. As shown in Fig. 3 , CL intensity decreased with the increase of the concentration of DHZS. The calibration curve for DHZS showed that there was a good linear relationship between relative CL intensity and the logarithm of DHZS concentration in the range from 1.0×10−7 mol/L to 7.0×10−5 mol/L with a correlation coefficient of 0.9994. The regression equation was ΔI =120.30 lgc (10−6 mol/L) +143.64. The limit of detection (LOD) as defined by IUPAC was 3.0×10−8 mol/L. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for seven repeated determination of 5.0×10−6 mol/L DHZS was 2.1%. The comparison of the proposed method with the reported methods [27–31] for the determination of DHZS was shown in Table 1 .

To confirm the selectivity of the proposed method, the experiments were investigated by using a series of solutions containing 1.0×10−6 mol/L DHZS and various amounts of interfering compounds. The tolerance limit was estimated to be less than ±5% of the error. The tolerable concentration ratios of foreign species to DHZS was over 1000-fold, K+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, NH4 +, F−, Cl−, Br−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, pyruvicacid, glucose, sucrose, EDTA, phthalate, salicylic acid and L-cysteine; 500-fold C2O4 2−, citric acid, creatinine, carbamide and starch; 100-fold Fe3+, Zn2+, reserpine, hydrochlorothiazide, pyridoxine, cysteine, riboflavin and hydroxylamine; 50-fold Cd2+, thiamine, L-tryptophan and rutoside; 10-fold Co2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, lactic acid, ascorbic acid and lactose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DHZS in Real Samples
ce:section.ce:para: To prove the applicability and feasibility of the proposed method, it was applied for the determination of DHZS in compound DHZS tablet. The samples were prepared and analyzed according to the general procedure described in the experimental section. The results were shown in Table 2 . The assay results agreed well enough with the nominal content. And recovery studies were carried out on real samples for the evaluation of the validity of the presented method. The recoveries for DHZS in the spiked samples ranged from 98% to 102%. Therefore, the proposed method was satisfactory and suitable for the determination of DHZS in pharmaceutical preparations.

The proposed method was applied to determine DHZS in spiked human urine and plasma samples. The results were listed in Table 3 . The recoveries of DHZS contents in the urine sample were 99 and 102%, in the plasma were 97 and 103% which demonstrated the accuracy of the proposed method in determining DHZS in urine and plasma samples.
To explore the mechanism of CL reaction, the CL spectra, FL spectra, UV–vis spectra and the effect of free radical scavengers were studied.

The CL spectra of KMnO4-HSO3 −, KMnO4-HSO3 −-HCNs and KMnO4-HSO3 −-HCNs-DHZS were measured using high-energy cutoff filters of various wavelengths (Fig. 4A). It was difficult to obtain the CL signal from the reaction of KMnO4 and HSO3 − due to it was very weak. Two main emission bands were observed in KMnO4-HSO3 −-HCNs and KMnO4-HSO3 −-HCNs-DHZS systems. The first band locating around 500nm was coincident with the PL spectra of HCNs (Fig. S1) and showed the luminophor in these systems was excited HCNs (HCNs*). The other emission band located after 620nm, which was in accordance with the emission from excited Mn (II) in the acidic permanganate CL system [23,36]. The present of DHZS in KMnO4-HSO3 −-HCNs system only had a decrease in CL intensity, and this indicated that the CL spectra were independent of DHZS. Fig. 4B showed that the PL emission wavelength of the KMnO4-HSO3 −-HCNs and KMnO4-HSO3 −-HCNs-DHZS systems was at 500nm, and PL intensity of KMnO4-HSO3 −-HCNs system decreased clearly in the presence of DHZS. It was further demonstrated that HCNs was the luminophor of the KMnO4-HSO3 −-HCNs system and DHZS only inhibited the luminous intensity.

Fig. S3 showed the UV–vis spectra of reaction systems. KMnO4 had a characteristic absorption peak at 525nm. After respective adding HSO3 −, HCNs or DHZS to KMnO4 solution, the color of KMnO4 gradually faded away, and the intensity of the characteristic absorption at 525nm decreased, which demonstrated that the different degrees of reaction took place between they and KMnO4. In the KMnO4-HSO3 −-HCNs and KMnO4-HSO3 −-HCNs-DHZS system, the characteristic absorption peak of KMnO4 at 525nm almost disappeared, and the absorption of HCNs or DHZS was different from the individual absorption, which showed that HCNs or DHZS was involved in the reaction of KMnO4 and HSO3 −. And the DHZS exhibited a certain reductibility in the reaction with acidic KMnO4.

Different radical scavengers on the CL intensity of KMnO4-HSO3 −-HCNs system were used to monitor the possible intermediates, and the results were listed in Table S1. Ascorbic acid, a classical reducing agent, was generally used as a free radical scavenger. About 90.5% of CL intensity was inhibited by 1.0×10−4 mol/L ascorbic acid due to its dehydrogenation by OH, O2 −. This supported the radical pathway in the KMnO4-HSO3 −-HCNs system. The OH radical was one of the most potent oxidizer and apparently played an important role in CL of the KMnO4-HSO3 −-HCNs system. Thiourea was commonly used as OH radical scavenger, and the restraint effect of 1.0×10−3 mol/L thiourea for 60.6% CL system indicated the generation of OH radical. Mannitol was used to study the effect of O2 – radicals, and added 1.0×10−3 mol/L mannitol to the CL system, the CL intensity decreased 40.5%, which confirmed that O2 – was also intermediate in the CL process. Sodium azide was used in this system, which physically quenched 1O2. The addition of 0.01mol/L NaN3 quenched the CL intensity about 10%, although the percentage of inhibitory of NaN3 was relative low, the 1O2 generation could not excluded.

Based on the above results, the mechanism of the CL reaction enhanced by HCNs was summarized in Scheme 1 . The OH and O2 – radicals were generated during the reaction of KMnO4 with HSO3 −. HCNs could react with these free radicals to form positively charged HCNs (HCNs +) and negatively charged HCNs (HCNs −), the electron-transfer annihilation of HCNs − and HCNs + formed the excited-state HCNs (HCNs⁎), which returned to the ground state by photon emission [16]. So HCNs* acted as the final emitter in the system. Also, O2 – was unstable in aqueous solution initiating the reactive oxygen chain reaction, which leads to the generation of the (1O2)2 ⁎ [37]. Then, HCNs could accept the energy from (1O2)2 ⁎, generating HCNs⁎, which returned to the ground state and released photon. Meanwhile, the emission spectrum of the excited sulfur dioxide (SO2 ⁎) from the reaction of KMnO4 with HSO3 − was within the range of 300–550nm [38,39], which overlapped well with the excitation spectrum of HCNs (Fig. SA, curve b). And a CL resonance energy transfer (CERT) occurred between SO2* (donor) and HCNs (acceptor), which produced HCNs⁎ [40], and a strong light emission obtained when the HCNs⁎ returned back to its ground state level. Furthermore, the O2 – were competitively consumed by DHZS, a 1,4-dihydrazinophthalazine sulfate, leading to the decrease of HCNs* and inhibition of CL intensity [29,41].

In this work, a novel CL system of HCNs-enhanced KMnO4-HSO3 − was established and this system was successfully applied to the detection of DHZS in pharmaceutical preparations and human fluid samples with satisfactory accuracy and precision. The CL emission of this system could be attributed to the chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer and the electron-transfer annihilation, which obtained a higher quantum efficiency and allowed its sensitive determination for DHZS. DHZS consumed the superoxide ion, and results in the decrease of CL intensity. This investigation not only further understand the unique property of carbon nanodots but also brought a broad application of the famous KMnO4-HSO3 − CL system.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi (2013011013-3); Undergraduate Training Program for Innovation and Entrepreneurship of Colleges and Universities of Nation (201610118003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2017.04.009.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strategies in liquid-phase chemiluminescence and their applications in bioassay
Application of chemiluminescence in the analysis of wastewaters—a review
A review on applications of chemiluminescence detection in food analysis

Nanomaterial-based chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer: a strategy to develop new analytical methods
Silicon-hybrid carbon dots strongly enhance the chemiluminescence of luminol
Synthesis and unique photoluminescence properties of nitrogen-rich quantum dots and their applications
Silicon quantum dots for biological applications
Sulfur-doped porous carbons: synthesis and applications
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots: a facile and general preparation method, photoluminescence investigation, and imaging applications
Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
Analytical and bioanalytical applications of carbon dots
A review on syntheses, properties, characterization and bioanalytical applications of fluorescent carbon dots
Analytical applications of chemiluminescence systems assisted by carbon nanostructures
Enhancement of ultraweak chemiluminescence from reaction of hydrogen peroxide and bisulfite by water-soluble carbon nanodots
Introducing confinement effects into ultraweak chemiluminescence for an improved sensitivity
Amino acids as the source for producing carbon nanodots: microwave assisted one-step synthesis, intrinsic photoluminescence property and intense chemiluminescence enhancement
Strong enhancement of the chemiluminescence of the cerium (IV)-thiosulfate reaction by carbon dots, and its application to the sensitive determination of dopamine
A chemiluminescence technique for measuring atmospheric trace concentrations of sulfur dioxide
The chemiluminescence of the SO2 oxidation and its application in atmospheric chemistry
